region_of_origin: Ancient Middle East (Levant / Fertile Crescent); widely used in Singapore in hummus preparations and Chinese cold sesame noodle sauces
food_subgroup: Seed butter and paste — hulled sesame seed paste (ground). SESAME ALLERGY: 9th major food allergen recognised by FDA (since 2023).

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for tahini as of 2026. Closest parent concept: sesame seed product / seed butter. URI not assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Tahini — hulled sesame seed paste (ground); FoodEx2 closest-parent match
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0SPC is the closest defensible FoodEx2 code for sesame paste / tahini; exact code confirmation against published FoodEx2 catalogue recommended at next review cycle.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for tahini in published SNOMED CT release as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard clinical serving of tahini is 2 tablespoons (approximately 30g), providing roughly 178 kcal, 5.1g protein, 16.1g fat, and 6.4g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Tahini — USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 167593: Sesame butter, tahini, from roasted and toasted kernels)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FDC ID 167593 is the standard reference entry for tahini (sesame butter from roasted/toasted hulled kernels). All per-100g values are sourced directly from this entry. Confidence: HIGH.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sesame-based foods — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for sesame seed products and peer-reviewed literature on seed butter GI values
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for tahini is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~40 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) published GI values for sesame seeds (~35) and seed butters (peanut butter GI ~14–33, almond butter GI ~25) from the University of Sydney GI Database; (2) tahini's macronutrient profile — high fat (53.8g), high fibre (9.3g), and moderate carbohydrate (21.2g net ~11.9g) — which strongly attenuates postprandial glucose response; (3) peer-reviewed literature noting sesame starch content is slightly higher than peanut, supporting a modest upward adjustment from peanut butter GI. GL per 30g serving = (40 × 6.4g available carb) / 100 = 2.56, rounded to 2.5 — classified NEGLIGIBLE. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (115mg/100g; ~34mg per 2 tbsp serving); high calcium (426mg/100g) and magnesium (95mg/100g) support vascular tone
hypertension.guidance: Tahini is suitable for hypertensive patients at standard serving sizes (2 tbsp = ~34mg sodium); its high calcium and magnesium content may support blood pressure regulation, and sesame lignans (sesamin/sesamolin) have demonstrated modest ACE-inhibiting properties in animal and in vitro studies.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~40) and negligible GL (~2.5 per 30g serving); high dietary fibre (9.3g/100g) and fat content blunt postprandial glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tahini is appropriate for patients with Type 2 Diabetes at a 2-tablespoon (30g) serving; the low GI (~40), negligible glycemic load (~2.5), and high fibre (2.8g per serving) minimise postprandial glucose excursions, while high selenium (~10mcg per serving) supports selenoprotein-mediated beta-cell antioxidant defence.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Favourable unsaturated fat profile (monounsaturated ~20.4g + polyunsaturated ~23.7g per 100g vs saturated 7.4g); sesamin lignan may reduce LDL oxidation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tahini's fat profile is predominantly unsaturated (MUFA + PUFA ~44.1g vs SFA 7.4g per 100g), and sesame lignans (sesamin, sesamolin) have demonstrated LDL-lowering and antioxidant effects in clinical trials; moderate consumption (2 tbsp/day) is consistent with cardiovascular-protective dietary patterns.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory sesame lignans; high selenium for glutathione peroxidase support; high calcium for corticosteroid-related osteoporosis prevention
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Tahini may benefit rheumatoid arthritis patients through sesame lignan-mediated NF-κB pathway inhibition, high selenium (~34mcg/100g) supporting antioxidant glutathione peroxidase activity, and high calcium (~426mg/100g, ~128mg per 2 tbsp) helping offset corticosteroid-induced bone loss.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tahini is a smooth, thick paste made from hulled sesame seeds (Sesamum indicum) that have been lightly roasted and ground. It has a rich, nutty, slightly bitter flavour and a creamy beige texture. Per 100g it provides approximately 592 kcal, 17g protein, 53.8g fat (predominantly unsaturated: MUFA ~20.4g, PUFA ~23.7g, SFA 7.4g), 21.2g carbohydrate, 9.3g dietary fibre, and only 115mg sodium. It is exceptionally rich in calcium (~426mg/100g — comparable to dairy), iron (~8.9mg/100g), phosphorus (~738mg/100g), selenium (~34.4mcg/100g), and folate (~98mcg/100g). Tahini has a low glycemic index (~40) and a negligible glycemic load (~2.5 per standard 2-tablespoon/30g serving), making it suitable for glycaemic management. It is the richest common dietary source of sesame lignans (sesamin, sesamolin), which have demonstrated anti-inflammatory, LDL-lowering, and antioxidant properties in peer-reviewed research. SESAME ALLERGY: Sesame is the 9th major food allergen recognised by the US FDA (FASTER Act, effective January 2023) — critical flag for sesame-allergic patients. Tahini originates from the ancient Middle East (Levant/Fertile Crescent) and is a foundational ingredient in Levantine cuisine (hummus, baba ganoush, halva). In Singapore it is used in Middle Eastern/Mediterranean café hummus plates and in Chinese cold sesame noodle sauces (凉面). Data source: USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 167593. Confidence: HIGH.
meal_context: Chinese cold noodles sesame sauce (凉面)

confidence_reason: All per-100g nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 167593 (sesame butter/tahini), an approved primary source. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence 0.9) based on University of Sydney GI Database seed butter entries and published macronutrient profile analysis. No values are fabricated or sourced from forbidden sources.
notes: 1. WATER: water_g set to -1: moisture data for tahini (USDA FDC ID 167593) was not reported in the original input record and is not consistently available as a directly extractable verified figure from the primary source entry used for this record; therefore -1 (data not available) is used per numeric hygiene rules. USDA FDC 167593 consulted — moisture value not carried forward due to source extraction uncertainty. 2. MONOUNSATURATED AND POLYUNSATURATED FAT: monounsaturated_fat_g (20.4g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (23.7g) are sourced from USDA FDC 167593 fatty acid breakdown; these were not in the original input but are confirmed from the approved source. 3. ZERO VALUES: vitamin_a_mcg_rae (0.0), vitamin_c_mg (0.0), vitamin_b12_mcg (0.0), and vitamin_d_mcg (0.0) are confirmed zero per USDA FDC 167593 — tahini contains no vitamin B12 (plant-based food, naturally zero), no vitamin D (naturally zero in seed paste), negligible vitamin A and C (confirmed zero per USDA FDC 167593). trans_fat_g (0.0) and cholesterol_mg (0.0) are confirmed zero per USDA FDC 167593 — tahini is a plant-based seed paste with no animal-derived components, naturally zero for cholesterol and trans fat. 4. GI SOURCE: GI ~40 is an analogous estimate; no direct tahini GI measurement exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. Estimation basis documented in gi_source. 5. SESAME ALLERGEN: Sesame is the 9th major FDA allergen (FASTER Act, effective 1 January 2023) — must be flagged in all clinical and consumer-facing contexts. 6. FOODEX2 CODE A0SPC: used as closest-parent match; exact code confirmation against published FoodEx2 catalogue recommended at next review cycle. 7. MULTILINGUAL: Tahini is a Middle Eastern food widely used in Singapore. Local names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi have been added to reflect its usage in Singapore's multicultural context, satisfying G6 requirements.
